title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis [PDF]

## **Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis**

**Prafulla Dhariwal** _[⇤]_ **Alex Nichol** _[⇤]_ OpenAI OpenAI `prafulla@openai.com alex@openai.com`

## **Abstract**

We show that diffusion models can achieve image sample quality superior to the current state-of-the-art generative models. We achieve this on unconditional image synthesis by finding a better architecture through a series of ablations. For conditional image synthesis, we further improve sample quality with classifier guidance: a simple, compute-efficient method for trading off diversity for fidelity using gradients from a classifier. We achieve an FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128, 4.59 on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256, and 7.72 on ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512, and we match BigGAN-deep even with as few as 25 forward passes per sample, all while maintaining better coverage of the distribution. Finally, we find that classifier guidance combines well with upsampling diffusion models, further improving FID to 3.94 on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256 and 3.85 on ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512.

## **1 Introduction**

Figure 1: Selected samples from our best ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512 model (FID 3.85)

Over the past few years, generative models have gained the ability to generate human-like natural language [9], high-quality synthetic images [8, 34, 57] and highly diverse human speech and music [70, 17]. These models can be used in a variety of ways, such as generating images from text prompts [78, 56] or learning useful feature representations [18, 10]. While these models are already capable

> _⇤_ Equal contribution

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

of producing realistic images and sound, there is still much room for improvement beyond the current state-of-the-art, and better generative models could have wide-ranging impacts on graphic design, games, music production, and countless other fields.

GANs [25] currently hold the state-of-the-art on most image generation tasks [8, 74, 34] as measured by sample quality metrics such as FID [29], Inception Score [61] and Precision [38]. However, some of these metrics do not fully capture diversity, and it has been shown that GANs capture less diversity than state-of-the-art likelihood-based models [57, 49, 48]. Furthermore, GANs are often difficult to train, collapsing without carefully selected hyperparameters and regularizers [8, 47, 7]. While GANs hold the state-of-the-art, their drawbacks make them difficult to scale and apply to new domains. As a result, much work has been done to achieve GAN-like sample quality with likelihood-based models [22, 57, 31, 48, 12]. While these models capture more diversity and are typically easier to scale and train than GANs, they still fall short in terms of visual fidelity. Furthermore, except for VAEs, sampling from these models is slower than GANs in terms of wall-clock time.

Diffusion models are a class of likelihood-based models which have recently been shown to produce high-quality images [63, 66, 31, 49] while offering desirable properties such as distribution coverage, a stationary training objective, and easy scalability. These models generate samples by gradually removing noise from a signal, and their training objective can be expressed as a reweighted variational lower-bound [31]. This class of models already holds the state-of-the-art [67] on CIFAR-10 [37], but still lags behind GANs on difficult generation datasets like LSUN and ImageNet. We hypothesize that this gap exists for at least two reasons: first, that the model architectures used by recent GAN literature have been heavily explored and refined; second, that GANs are able to trade off diversity for fidelity, producing high quality samples but not covering the whole distribution. We aim to bring these benefits to diffusion models, first by improving model architecture and then by devising a scheme for trading off diversity for fidelity.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we give a brief background of diffusion models based on Ho et al. [31] and the improvements from Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Song et al. [64], and we describe our evaluation setup. In Section 3, we introduce simple architecture improvements that give a substantial boost to FID. In Section 4, we describe a method for using gradients from a classifier to guide a diffusion model during sampling. Finally, in Section 5 we show that models with our improved architecture achieve state-of-the-art on unconditional image synthesis tasks, and with classifier guidance achieve state-of-the-art on conditional image synthesis.

## **2 Background**

In this section, we provide a brief overview of diffusion models. For a more detailed mathematical description, we refer the reader to Appendix C. On a high level, diffusion models sample from a distribution by reversing a gradual noising process. In particular, sampling starts with noise _xT_ and produces gradually less-noisy samples _xT −_ 1 _, xT −_ 2 _, ..._ until reaching a final sample _x_ 0. In particular, a diffusion model learns to produce a slightly more “denoised” _xt−_ 1 from _xt_ . Ho et al. [31] parameterize this model using a function _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) which predicts the noise component of a noisy sample _xt_ . To train this function, each sample in a minibatch is produced by randomly drawing a data sample _x_ 0, a timestep _t_ , and noise _✏_ , which together give rise to a noised sample _xt_ (Equation 3, Appendix C). The training objective is then _||✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) _− ✏||_[2] , i.e. a simple mean-squared error loss between the true noise and the predicted noise (Equation 12, Appendix C).

Ho et al. [31] show that, under reasonable assumptions, we can then model the denoising distribution _p✓_ ( _xt−_ 1 _|xt_ ) of _xt−_ 1 given _xt_ as a diagonal Gaussian _N_ ( _xt−_ 1; _µ✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) _,_ ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ )), where the mean _µ✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) can be calculated as a function of _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) (Equation 13, Appendix C). Ho et al. [31] observe that the simple mean-squared error objective, _L_ simple, works better in practice than the actual variational lower bound _L_ vlb that can be derived from interpreting the denoising diffusion model as a VAE. They also note that training with this objective and using their corresponding sampling procedure is equivalent to the denoising score matching model from Song and Ermon [65], who use Langevin dynamics to sample from a denoising model trained with multiple noise levels to produce high quality image samples. We often use “diffusion models” as shorthand to refer to both classes of models.
Following the breakthrough work of Song and Ermon [65] and Ho et al. [31], several recent papers have proposed improvements to diffusion models. Nichol and Dhariwal [49] find that fixing

2

the variance ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) to a constant as done in Ho et al. [31] is sub-optimal for sampling with fewer diffusion steps, and propose to parameterize ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) as a neural network whose output _v_ is interpolated as ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) = exp( _v_ log _βt_ + (1 _− v_ ) log _β_[˜] _t_ ). Here, _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ (Equation 5, Appendix C) are the variances in Ho et al. [31] corresponding to upper and lower bounds for the reverse process variances. Additionally, Nichol and Dhariwal [49] propose a hybrid objective for training both _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) and ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) using the weighted sum _L_ simple + _λL_ vlb. Learning the reverse process variances with their hybrid objective allows sampling with fewer steps without much drop in sample quality. We adopt this objective and parameterization, and use it throughout our experiments.

Song et al. [64] propose DDIM, which formulates an alternative non-Markovian noising process that has the same forward marginals as DDPM, but allows producing different reverse samplers by changing the variance of the reverse noise. By setting this noise to 0, they provide a way to turn any model _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) into a deterministic mapping from latents to images, and find that this provides an alternative way to sample with fewer steps. We adopt this sampling approach when using fewer than 50 sampling steps, since Nichol and Dhariwal [49] found it to be beneficial in this regime.

**Sample Quality Metrics** : For comparing sample quality across models, we perform quantitative evaluations using the following metrics. While these metrics are often used in practice and correspond well with human judgement, they are not a perfect proxy, and finding better metrics for sample quality evaluation is still an open problem.

We use FID [29] as our default metric for overall sample quality comparisons as it captures both fidelity and diversity and has been the de facto standard metric for state-of-the-art generative models [33, 34, 8, 31]. We use Precision and Recall [38] as proxies for separately measuring fidelity and diversity, respectively. We include sFID [48] as a metric that better captures spatial relationships than FID, and also include Inception Score (IS) [61] as another proxy for fidelity. When comparing against other methods, we re-compute these metrics using public samples or models whenever possible. This is for two reasons: first, some papers [33, 34, 31] compare against arbitrary subsets of the training set which are not readily available; and second, subtle implementation differences can affect the resulting FID values [51]. For consistent comparisons, we use the full training set as the reference batch [29, 8], and evaluate metrics for all models using the same codebase.

## **3 Architecture Improvements**

Ho et al. [31] adopted the UNet architecture [58] for diffusion models, which Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [32] found to substantially improve sample quality over the previous architectures [65, 39] used for denoising score matching. The UNet model uses a stack of residual layers and downsampling convolutions, followed by a stack of residual layers with upsampling convolutions, with skip connections connecting the layers with the same spatial size. In addition, they use a global attention layer at the 16 _⇥_ 16 resolution with a single head, and add a projection of the timestep embedding into each residual block. Song et al. [67] found that further changes to the UNet architecture improved performance on the CIFAR-10 [37] and CelebA-64 [40] datasets. We show the same result on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128, finding that architecture can indeed give a substantial boost to sample quality on a much larger and more diverse datasets at a higher resolution.

We explore the following architectural changes: increasing depth versus width, holding model size relatively constant; increasing the number of attention heads; using attention at 32 _⇥_ 32, 16 _⇥_ 16, and 8 _⇥_ 8 resolutions rather than only at 16 _⇥_ 16; using the BigGAN [8] residual block for upsampling and downsampling the activations, following [67]; and finally; rescaling residual connections with ~~_p_~~ 12[,] following [67, 33, 34].

We train models with the above architecture changes on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128 and compare them on FID, evaluated at two different points of training, in Table 1. Aside from rescaling residual connections, all of the other modifications improve performance and have a positive compounding effect. On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1) we find that increased depth hurts training time most, so we opt not to use this change in further experiments. We also study other attention configurations that better match the Transformer architecture [72]. We try two configurations: constant attention heads, or constant channels per head. Table 2 shows our results, indicating that more heads or fewer channels per head improves FID. On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1), we see that 64 channels is best so we opt to use 64 channels per head as our default. We note that this choice also better matches modern transformer architectures, and is on par with our other configurations in terms of final FID.
Table 2: Ablation of attention heads. More heads or lower channels per heads both improve FID. The base model was a smaller version of the best model from Table 1.

We also experiment with a layer [49] that we refer to as adaptive group normalization (AdaGN), which incorporates the timestep and class embedding into each residual block after a group normalization operation [75], similar to adaptive instance norm [33] and FiLM [54]. We define this layer as AdaGN( _h, y_ ) = _ys_ GroupNorm( _h_ )+ _yb_ , where _h_ is the intermediate activations of the residual block following the first convolution, and _y_ = [ _ys, yb_ ] is obtained from a linear projection of the timestep and class embedding. We had already seen AdaGN improve our earliest diffusion models, and so had included it by default in all our runs. We explicitly ablate this choice (Table 6, Appendix A.1), and find that FID becomes worse by 2.02 when we remove the adaptive group normalization layer.

variable width with 2 residual blocks per resolution, multiple heads with 64 channels per head, attention at 32, 16 and 8 resolutions, BigGAN residual blocks for up and downsampling, and adaptive group normalization for injecting timestep and class embeddings into residual blocks.

## **4**

In addition to employing well designed architectures, GANs for conditional image synthesis [45, 8] make heavy use of class labels. This often takes the form of class-conditional normalization statistics [20, 14] as well as discriminators with heads explicitly designed to behave like classifiers _p_ ( _y|x_ ) [46]. As further evidence that class information is crucial to the success of these models, Lucic et al. [42] find that it is helpful to generate synthetic labels when working in a label-limited regime. Given this observation for GANs, it makes sense to explore different ways to condition diffusion models on class labels. We already incorporate class information into adaptive group normalization layers (Section 3). Here, we explore a different approach: exploiting a classifier _p_ ( _y|x_ ) to improve a diffusion generator. Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63] and Song et al. [67] show one way to achieve this, wherein a pre-trained diffusion model can be conditioned using the gradients of a classifier. In particular, we can train a classifier _pφ_ ( _y|xt, t_ ) on noisy images _xt_ , and then use gradients _rxt_ log _pφ_ ( _y|xt, t_ ) to guide the diffusion sampling process towards an arbitrary class label _y_ .

For class conditional diffusion sampling, we reproduce the derivation from Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63] in Appendix D.2. For DDIM, we perform a score-based derivation in Appendix D.3 inspired by Song et al. [67]. The resulting sampling algorithms we use for guidance are Algorithms 1 and 2 respectively. Both algorithms incorporate class information by adding the gradients of a classifier to each sampling step with an appropriate step size. In these algorithms, we choose the notation

To apply guidance to a large scale generative task, we train models on ImageNet. Our classifier architecture is simply the downsampling trunk of the UNet model with an attention pool [55] at the 8x8 layer to produce the final output. We train these classifiers on the same noising distribution as the corresponding diffusion model, and also add random crops to reduce overfitting.

In initial experiments with unconditional ImageNet models, we found it necessary to scale the classifier gradients by a constant factor larger than 1. When using a scale of 1, we observed that the classifier assigned reasonable probabilities (around 50%) to the desired classes for the final samples, but these samples did not match the intended classes upon visual inspection. Scaling up the classifier gradients remedied this problem, and the class probabilities from the classifier increased to nearly 100%. Figure 2 shows an example of this effect. We see that scaling the gradients beyond 1.0 smoothly trades off recall (a measure of diversity) for higher precision and IS (measures of fidelity). Since FID and sFID depend on both diversity and fidelity, their best values are obtained at an intermediate point. We also compare our guidance with the truncation trick from BigGAN (Figure 6, Appendix A.2). We find that classifier guidance is strictly better than BigGAN-deep when trading off FID for Inception Score. Less clear cut is the precision/recall trade-off, which shows that classifier guidance is only a better choice up until a certain precision threshold, after which point it cannot achieve better precision.

## **5 Results**

To evaluate our improved model architecture on unconditional image generation, we train separate diffusion models on three LSUN [77] classes: bedroom, horse, and cat. To evaluate classifier guidance, we train conditional diffusion models on the ImageNet [59] dataset at 128 _⇥_ 128, 256 _⇥_ 256, and 512 _⇥_ 512 resolution.

Table 4 summarizes our results. ADM refers to our **a** blated **d** iffusion **m** odel, and ADM-G additionally uses classifier **g** uidance. Our diffusion models can obtain the best FID on each task, and the best sFID on all but one task. With the improved architecture, we already obtain state-of-the-art image generation on LSUN and ImageNet 64 _⇥_ 64. For higher resolution ImageNet, we observe that classifier guidance allows our models to substantially outperform the best GANs. These models obtain perceptual quality similar to GANs, while maintaining a higher coverage of the distribution as measured by recall, and can even do so using only 25 sampling steps. We also evaluate the computational requirements for training our models (Table 10, Appendix B), and find that we can obtain competitive sample quality while using the same or less compute than the corresponding BigGAN-deep or StyleGAN2 model.

We also compare guidance to using a two-stage upsampling stack. Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Saharia et al. [60] train two-stage diffusion models by combining a low-resolution diffusion model with a corresponding upsampling diffusion model. In this approach, the upsampling model is trained to upsample images from the training set, and conditions on low-resolution images that are concatenated channel-wise to the model input using a simple interpolation (e.g. bilinear). During sampling, the low-resolution model produces a sample, and then the upsampling model is conditioned on this sample. This greatly improves FID on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256, but does not reach the same performance as state-of-the-art models like BigGAN-deep [49, 60], as seen in Table 4.

In Table 5, we show that guidance and upsampling improve sample quality along different axes. We use the **u** psampling stack from Nichol and Dhariwal [49] combined with our architecture improvements, which we refer to as ADM-U. While upsampling improves precision while keeping a high recall, guidance provides a knob to trade off diversity for much higher precision. We achieve the best FIDs by using guidance at a lower resolution before upsampling to a higher resolution, indicating that these approaches complement one another.

## **6 Related Work**

Score based generative models were introduced by Song and Ermon [66] as a way of modeling a data distribution using its gradients, and then sampling using Langevin dynamics [73]. Ho et al. [31] found a connection between this method and diffusion models [63], and achieved excellent sample quality by leveraging this connection. After this breakthrough work, many works followed up with

7

more promising results: Kong et al. [36] and Chen et al. [11] demonstrated that diffusion models work well for audio; Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [32] found that a GAN-like setup could improve samples from these models; Song et al. [67] explored ways to leverage techniques from stochastic differential equations to improve the sample quality obtained by score-based models; Song et al. [64] and Nichol and Dhariwal [49] proposed methods to improve sampling speed; Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Saharia et al. [60] demonstrated promising results on the difficult ImageNet generation task using upsampling diffusion models. Also related to diffusion models, and following the work of Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63], Goyal et al. [27] described a technique for learning a model with learned iterative generation steps, and found that it could achieve good image samples when trained with a likelihood objective.

Other generative techniques provide natural levers for this trade-off. Brock et al. [8] introduced the truncation trick for GANs, wherein the latent vector is sampled from a truncated normal distribution. They found that increasing truncation naturally led to a decrease in diversity but an increase in fidelity. More recently, Razavi et al. [57] proposed to use classifier rejection sampling to filter out bad samples from an autoregressive likelihood-based model, and found that this technique improved FID. DeVries et al. [16] found that filtering out low-density regions of the training set improves GAN training performance. Most likelihood-based models also allow for low-temperature sampling [1], which provides a natural way to emphasize modes of the data distribution (see Appendix I).

8

Other likelihood-based models have been shown to produce high-fidelity image samples. VQ-VAE [71] and VQ-VAE-2 [57] are autoregressive models trained on top of quantized latent codes, greatly reducing the computational resources required to train these models on large images. These models produce diverse and high quality images, but still fall short of GANs without expensive rejection sampling and special metrics to compensate for blurriness. DCTransformer [48] is a related method which relies on a more intelligent compression scheme. VAEs are another promising class of likelihood-based models, and recent methods such as NVAE [69] and VDVAE [12] have successfully been applied to difficult image generation domains. Energy-based models are another class of likelihood-based models with a rich history [1, 13, 30]. Sampling from the EBM distribution is challenging, and Xie et al. [76] demonstrate that Langevin dynamics can be used to sample coherent images from these models. Du and Mordatch [19] further improve upon this approach, obtaining high quality images. More recently, Gao et al. [24] incorporate diffusion steps into an energy-based model, and find that doing so improves image samples from these models.

body of work [23, 53, 2] aims to optimize GAN latent spaces for text prompts using pre-trained CLIP [55] models. More similar to our work, Song et al. [67] uses a classifier to generate class-conditional CIFAR-10 images with a diffusion model. In some cases, classifiers can act as stand-alone generative models. For example, Santurkar et al. [62] demonstrate that a robust image classifier can be used as a stand-alone generative model, and Grathwohl et al. [28] train a model which is jointly a classifier and an energy-based model.

## **7 Limitations and Future Work**

While we believe diffusion models are an extremely promising direction for generative modeling, they are still slower than GANs at sampling time due to the use of multiple denoising steps (and therefore forward passes). Since our diffusion models are also larger than the competing GAN generators, each forward pass takes anywhere from 5-20 times longer too. A promising direction to reduce this latency gap is Luhman and Luhman [43], who explore a way to distill the DDIM sampling process into a single step model. The samples from the single step model are not yet competitive with GANs, but are much better than previous single-step likelihood-based models. Future work in this direction might be able to completely close the sampling speed gap between diffusion models and GANs without sacrificing image quality.

Unlike GANs, Flows, and VAEs, diffusion models do not learn an explicit latent representation. While DDIM provides a way to encode images into an implicit latent space, it is unclear how semantically meaningful this latent representation is compared to those of other model classes. This could make it difficult to use diffusion models for representation learning or image editing applications.

from the gradients of a classification function. This could be used to condition an image generator with a text caption using a noisy version of CLIP [55], similar to recent methods that guide GANs using text prompts [23, 53, 2]. Our proposed classifier guidance technique is currently limited to labeled datasets. In the future, our method could be extended to unlabeled data by clustering samples to produce synthetic labels [42] or by training discriminative models to use for guidance. This also suggests that large unlabeled datasets could be leveraged in the future to pre-train powerful diffusion models that can later be improved by using a classifier with desirable properties.

## **8 Societal Impact**

Our proposed technique makes generative models more accessible in terms of compute costs, especially because new classifiers can be trained and used on top of existing high-quality diffusion models. While we believe this is generally a benefit of these models, it could also have negative societal implications. For example, cheaper generative models could enable bad actors to generate fake news, propaganda images, or doctored photos. Additionally, the wide-spread deployment of these models could displace jobs in art, graphic design, animation, and photography. One could imagine, however, that democratizing generative models could also have positive impacts in the long run, creating new types of jobs such as generative photo editing. Intentionally deceitful generated

9

images are a more direct concern, and detecting and mitigating propaganda and fake news based on generative models is an ongoing area of research [4, 3, 5].

## **9 Conclusion**

We have shown that diffusion models, a class of likelihood-based models with a stationary training objective, can obtain better sample quality than state-of-the-art GANs. Our improved architecture is sufficient to achieve this on unconditional image generation tasks, and our classifier guidance technique allows us to do so on class-conditional tasks. In the latter case, we find that the scale of the classifier gradients can be adjusted to trade off diversity for fidelity. These guided diffusion models can reduce the sampling time gap between GANs and diffusion models, although diffusion models still require multiple forward passes during sampling. Finally, by combining guidance with upsampling, we can further improve sample quality on high-resolution conditional image synthesis.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and potentially landmark contribution to the field of generative modeling, demonstrating exceptional clarity in its motivation and structure. The introduction is particularly effective, framing the performance gap between diffusion models and GANs around two key, testable hypotheses regarding architecture and the diversity-fidelity trade-off, which provides a strong narrative for the work. The abstract's bold claim is immediately substantiated with specific, state-of-the-art FID scores, which establishes high expectations. The most significant issue, however, lies in the as-yet-unseen substantiation of its core technical contributions. While the paper promises a "better architecture" and a novel "classifier guidance" technique, the introduction does not sufficiently hint at the novelty or mechanistic underpinnings of these components. The ultimate impact of this work will hinge on a detailed explanation of *why* these specific changes are so effective and whether the architectural improvements represent genuinely new insights for diffusion models or are a skillful application of principles from the GAN literature. Furthermore, the heavy emphasis on FID, while impressive, raises the question of whether a more holistic evaluation will be presented to fully justify the strong claim that diffusion models have "beaten" GANs across all meaningful dimensions of image synthesis.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a significant pivot in the field of generative modeling by demonstrating that diffusion models can surpass GANs in image synthesis—a claim supported by impressive state-of-the-art results on ImageNet datasets. The quality of the work is exceptionally high, characterized by a rigorous systematic evaluation of architectural bottlenecks and the clever introduction of "classifier guidance" to address the fidelity-diversity trade-off that previously favored GANs. The clarity is commendable; the authors provide a transparent roadmap of their improvements, bridging the gap between score-based modeling and variational inference. However, a significant issue is the heavy reliance on the Appendix (C) for fundamental equations which, while maintaining narrative flow, makes the paper feel slightly fragmented for a first-time reader. Additionally, while the paper addresses sampling efficiency (matching BigGAN-deep in 25 passes), the inference costs and the "classifier dependency" for guidance could pose practical deployment challenges compared to the single-pass nature of GANs. Overall, this is a foundational manuscript that effectively shifts the paradigm toward diffusion-based architectures.

comments.title: Notation confusion and errors in DDIM formulas (Algorithm 2)
comments.quote: $x_{t-1} \leftarrow \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}} \left( \frac{x_t - \sqrt{1-\alpha_t} \hat{\epsilon}}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1-\alpha_{t-1}} \hat{\epsilon}$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 contains multiple notation errors regarding the DDIM update step and guidance formula. The standard DDIM formulation (Song et al.) relies on $\bar{\alpha}$ (the cumulative product of variances) to maintain the marginal distribution $q(x_t|x_0)$. However, the algorithm as written uses $\alpha_t$ and $\alpha_{t-1}$ (individual step variances). This discrepancy extends to the guided noise prediction $\hat{\epsilon}$, where $\sqrt{1-\alpha_t}$ is used instead of the $\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}$ mentioned in the paper's own summary. Given that $\alpha_t$ approaches 1 while $\bar{\alpha}_t$ approaches 0 as $t \to T$, using the single-step $\alpha_t$ would lead to a significant magnitude error in both the $x_0$ reconstruction and the gradient scaling, making the algorithm technically incorrect for the DDIM framework.

comments.title: Ambiguity and Magnitude Sensitivity in Guided Mean Sampling
comments.quote: sample from $N(\mu + s \Sigma \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t), \Sigma)$
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the mean is shifted by $s \Sigma \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$. There is ambiguity regarding which $\Sigma$ is used here. While theoretically grounded in a Gaussian approximation, the practical sensitivity of the scale $s$ depends heavily on whether $\Sigma$ refers to the learned interpolated variance $\Sigma_\theta$ or a fixed schedule $\sigma^2$. Since $\Sigma$ is often very small, the inclusion of this factor within the shift means the effective gradient step could be extremely small unless $s$ is very large. This makes the reproducibility of results in Figure 2 and Table 3 (which cite scales like 1.0 and 10.0) difficult without further clarification on the exact multiplier used.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Table 3 Data Columns
comments.quote: Table 3: Effect of classifier guidance on sample quality... |4 6 8 10 12 14 16||FID sFID IS precision recall|||||
comments.explanation: Table 3 in the passage contains significantly corrupted or redundant layout data. While the first few rows show comparisons between unconditional and conditional models with guidance scales of 1.0 and 10.0, the bottom of the table includes a sequence of numbers (4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16) and placeholder headers that do not match the columns or the metrics described in the text. Furthermore, the text mentions a 'trade-off in sample fidelity versus diversity' and references Figure 3 for the gradient scale variation, but the table's structure makes it difficult to verify the specific 'intermediate' scale mentioned as optimal.

comments.title: Conflicting Guidance formulas in Algorithm 2 and Summary
comments.quote: $\hat{\epsilon} \leftarrow \epsilon_\theta(x_t) - \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} s \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$
comments.explanation:
There is a discrepancy in how the guided noise prediction $\hat{\epsilon}$ is formulated for DDIM. The 'Key Equations' summary in the prompt defines $\hat{\epsilon}(x_t) = \epsilon_	heta(x_t) - \sqrt{1- ar{\alpha}_t} s 
abla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$. However, in Algorithm 2 (which represents the explicit method used in the paper), the line reads: $\hat{\epsilon} \leftarrow \epsilon_	heta(x_t) - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} 
abla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$. Note the usage of $\alpha_t$ (the marginal variance of a single step) versus $ ar{\alpha}_t$ (the cumulative product of variances). In the context of diffusion literature (Song et al. DDIM), the score function $
abla \log p(x_t)$ is usually related to the noise prediction $\epsilon$ via the factor $\sqrt{1- ar{\alpha}_t}$. If the algorithm intended to use the score-matching identity, the $ ar{\alpha}_t$ in the summary is more likely correct, while the $\alpha_t$ in the OCR-extracted Algorithm 2 looks like a potential typo or misread of the bar symbol. This is critical for implementation as it changes the scale of guidance at every timestep.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in guided mean sampling
comments.quote: $x_{t-1}$ sample from $N(\mu + s \Sigma \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t), \Sigma)$
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the shifted mean for classifier guidance is given as $\mu + s \Sigma \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$. Earlier in Section 2, the paper specifies that the denoising distribution $p_\theta(x_{t-1} | x_t)$ is modeled as a diagonal Gaussian with variance $\Sigma_\theta(x_t, t)$. Mathematically, if one treats the guidance as a product of distributions $p(x_{t-1}|x_t)p(y|x_t)$, the gradient of the log-probability of the classifier should indeed be scaled by the variance $\Sigma$ of the transition distribution. However, the authors define $\Sigma$ as an interpolation between $\beta_t$ and $\tilde{\beta}_t$. In many diffusion implementations (like the original DDPM), the 'simple' variance $\sigma^2$ is used. Using the learned $\Sigma_\theta$ (which can be quite small) as the multiplier for the guidance gradient might lead to very different effective guidance scales compared to using a fixed variance schedule. While theoretically sound under a Gaussian approximation, the practical sensitivity of 's' depends heavily on which $\Sigma$ is used, and the text is slightly ambiguous on whether $\Sigma$ in Algorithm 1 refers to the learned interpolated variance or the predefined noise schedule.

comments.title: Notation confusion between alpha and alpha-bar in DDIM
comments.quote: $x_{t-1} \leftarrow \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}} \left( \frac{x_t - \sqrt{1-\alpha_t} \hat{\epsilon}}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1-\alpha_{t-1}} \hat{\epsilon}$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 as extracted via OCR contains several potential notation errors regarding the DDIM update step. The standard DDIM formula (Song et al., 2020) relies on $\bar{\alpha}_t$ (the cumulative product) to define the deterministic mapping. The algorithm text in the passage shows $\alpha_t$ and $\alpha_{t-1}$ (individual step variances) in positions where $\bar{\alpha}_t$ is conventionally required to maintain the marginal distribution $q(x_t|x_0)$. For example, the term $(x_t - \sqrt{1-\alpha_t}\hat{\epsilon})/\sqrt{\alpha_t}$ is intended to be the predicted $x_0$. If $\alpha_t$ is the single-step variance (typically $\approx 0.999$), this formula will not correctly reconstruct $x_0$. It is highly probable that the OCR missed the bar notation over the $\alpha$ symbols. While a reader familiar with DDIM can infer the intent, the symbols as written in the passage are technically incorrect for the DDIM algorithm.

comments.title: Error in Classifier Guided DDIM Sampling Equation
comments.quote: \hat{\epsilon} \leftarrow \epsilon_\theta(x_t) - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, the update for the modified noise prediction $\hat{\epsilon}$ includes a term $-\sqrt{1-\alpha_t} \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$. However, the DDIM formulation relies on $\bar{\alpha}$ (the cumulative product of alphas), not the single-step $\alpha_t$. Looking at the Paper Summary provided in the context, the 'Key Equations' section correctly lists the formula as utilizing $\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}$. Given that $\alpha_t$ typically approaches 1 while $\bar{\alpha}_t$ approaches 0 as $t \to T$, using the single-step $\alpha_t$ here would lead to a significant magnitude error in the gradient scaling, potentially making the guidance ineffective or unstable as $t$ changes. This appears to be an OCR or notation error in the provided algorithm block that contradicts the correct formulation in the summary.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Table 3 Data Columns
comments.quote: Table 3: Effect of classifier guidance on sample quality... |4 6 8 10 12 14 16||FID sFID IS precision recall|||||
comments.explanation: Table 3 in the passage contains significantly corrupted or redundant layout data. While the first few rows show comparisons between unconditional and conditional models with guidance scales of 1.0 and 10.0, the bottom of the table includes a sequence of numbers (4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16) and placeholder headers that do not match the columns or the metrics described in the text. Furthermore, the text mentions a 'trade-off in sample fidelity versus diversity' and references Figure 3 for the gradient scale variation, but the values in the table for scales 1.0 and 10.0 for the conditional model show FID actually worsening (increasing from 4.59 to 9.11). While this aligns with the claim that FID peaks at an 'intermediate point,' the table's structure makes it difficult to verify the specific 'intermediate' scale mentioned as optimal.

comments.title: Potential Magnitude Mismatch in Guided Mean Update
comments.quote: sample from $N(\mu + s \Sigma \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t), \Sigma)$
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the mean of the Gaussian is shifted by $s \Sigma \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t)$. While theoretically grounded, earlier literature on score-based models and the authors' own 'Key Equations' summary suggest the variance term $\Sigma$ is crucial. However, if $\Sigma$ is the learned diagonal variance $\Sigma_\theta(x_t, t)$, it typically scales with $\beta_t$, which is quite small. The text notes in Section 4 that 'using a scale of 1... did not produce convincing samples,' necessitating a large $s$. If the $\Sigma$ factor is indeed included within the shift as written, the effective gradient step might be extremely small unless $s$ is very large (e.g., $s=100$ or $1000$). The text mentions scales like 1.0 and 10.0 in Figure 2 and Table 3. There is a slight ambiguity whether $s$ is a 'global' scale or if it's intended to compensate for the magnitude of $\Sigma$. If the reader assumes $s$ is the total multiplier, the inclusion of $\Sigma$ inside the product is a vital detail for reproducibility.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a significant and well-executed advancement in image synthesis by demonstrating that diffusion models can decisively outperform GANs in sample quality while maintaining strong diversity and training stability. The writing is clear, technically precise, and logically structured, with a strong motivation that effectively frames the limitations of GANs—particularly their training instability and poor distribution coverage—against the more stable and flexible framework of diffusion models. The core contributions—architectural improvements and the introduction of classifier guidance—are introduced with clarity and rigor, supported by compelling empirical results across multiple ImageNet resolutions. The FID scores reported, especially the 3.85 on 512×512 ImageNet with upsampling, represent a new state of the art and are highly persuasive. However, a key high-level concern is the reliance on FID as the primary metric without deeper qualitative analysis (e.g., human evaluation) or discussion of potential failure modes, such as overfitting to the Inception network or semantic implausibility in high-fidelity samples. Additionally, while classifier guidance is presented as a simple and effective mechanism for trading diversity for fidelity, the paper could better acknowledge the inherent tension between metric improvement and true data distribution modeling—particularly since guidance effectively steers sampling using external labels, raising questions about how much of the gain comes from the generative model itself versus the guiding classifier. Lastly, the abrupt truncation at the end of the provided text suggests missing key experimental details and analysis; ensuring full methodological transparency—especially regarding computational cost and sampling efficiency—will be critical for reproducibility and adoption. Overall, this is a strong, impactful paper that repositions diffusion models at the forefront of image generation, though clarity could be further enhanced with more nuanced discussion of trade-offs and limitations.

comments.title: Inconsistent FID values between Abstract and results in Table 3 and Table 4
comments.quote: We achieve an FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 128 × 128, 4.59 on ImageNet 256 × 256, and 7.72 on ImageNet 512 × 512, and we match BigGAN-deep even with as few as 25 forward passes per sample, all while maintaining better coverage of the distribution. Finally, we find that classifier guidance combines well with upsampling diffusion models, further improving FID to 3.94 on ImageNet 256 × 256 and 3.85 on ImageNet 512 × 512.
comments.explanation: The abstract presents two sets of results without clear distinction: standalone 'ADM-G' models (FID 4.59 at 256×256) and combined 'guidance + upsampling' models (FID 3.94 and 3.85). However, Table 3 only reports results up to FID 9.11 for conditional models and does not include any entries below 4.59, while Table 4 confirms the 4.59 and 7.72 values but omits the better upsampling results (3.94, 3.85), which appear only in Table 5. This creates a misleading impression that the best-reported model performance is not fully represented in the primary comparison table. The abstract's opening claim of 4.59 as a top result contradicts the later claim of 3.85 as better, yet the latter is not shown in the key result table. This undermines clarity and may lead readers to undervalue the paper’s strongest achievement. A unified results table including all configurations would resolve this.

comments.explanation: The AdaGN mechanism depends on y, which is derived from a projection of timestep and class embedding, but the projection process is underspecified. Are the embeddings concatenated before projection? Is the timestep encoded using sinusoidal positional encoding, as standard in diffusion models? While this is a known convention (e.g., in Ho et al. [31]), the omission reduces methodological transparency. Given that AdaGN ablation shows a 2.02 drop in FID, precise specification is essential for full reproducibility. Experts may infer the correct design, but clarity would benefit broader readership.

comments.title: Unclear meaning of 'ADM (dropout)' in Table 4 without definition

However, dropout is not mentioned in the methodology or ablation studies (Tables 1–2). The distinction between 'ADM' and 'ADM (dropout)' implies different configurations, but without explanation, it is unclear whether dropout improves performance or if the label is a misnomer. This ambiguity hampers interpretability and reproducibility, especially since performance varies across datasets.

comments.title: Insufficient justification and analysis for gradient scaling > 1 in classifier guidance
comments.quote: In initial experiments with unconditional ImageNet models, we found it necessary to scale the classifier gradients by a constant factor larger than 1. When using a scale of 1, we observed that the classifier assigned reasonable probabilities (around 50%) to the desired classes for the final samples, but these samples did not match the intended classes upon visual inspection.
comments.explanation: The paper justifies s > 1 empirically by noting improved visual alignment and higher classifier confidence, but does not examine *why* s = 1 fails. Possible explanations—such as classifier miscalibration on noisy inputs, signal decay over diffusion timesteps, or distribution mismatch—are not discussed. While the transformation p(y|x)^s is mathematically valid, the assumption that increased classifier confidence reflects true semantic correctness is circular. No external validation (e.g., human evaluation or a held-out classifier) supports this. Later works (e.g., GLIDE, DALL-E) confirm the empirical benefit, so the outcome is valid, but the lack of deeper analysis limits methodological insight.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing of DDIM sampler usage implies exclusivity to 25 steps

comments.explanation: The phrasing suggests that DDIM is used *only* when 25 steps are taken, implying a one-to-one mapping. However, DDIM is a sampler that can operate at any step count. The intended meaning is likely 'when sampling with 25 steps, we use the DDIM sampler', which is reasonable for speed. As written, it misrepresents the relationship between sampler and step count, potentially confusing readers about implementation choices. Context from Section 2 clarifies that DDIM is used for fewer than 50 steps, mitigating the issue, but the caption remains imprecise.

comments.title: Underspecified use of 'attention pool' in classifier architecture
comments.quote: Our classifier architecture is simply the downsampling trunk of the UNet model with an attention pool [55] at the 8x8 layer to produce the final output.
comments.explanation: The term 'attention pool' is not standard and lacks definition. While [55] refers to CLIP, which uses attention-based global pooling to aggregate spatial features, the mechanism—whether weighted average, learned query attention, or another variant—is not specified. This affects reproducibility, especially since classifier quality directly impacts guidance effectiveness. A more precise description (e.g., 'attention-based global pooling with a learnable query vector') would improve clarity. The reference helps, but within a standalone paper, the operation should be self-contained.

Possible reasons include classifier miscalibration on noisy inputs, signal-to-noise decay over timesteps, or mismatch between classifier training and diffusion dynamics. The phenomenon is important (it's central to guidance efficacy), and while the empirical justification is plausible, the lack of deeper analysis (even speculative) weakens the methodological grounding. comments.title: Vague Definition of AdaGN Layer Inputs and Preprocessing
comments.quote: y = [ _ys, yb_ ] is obtained from a linear projection of the timestep and class embedding.
comments.explanation: The AdaGN layer is defined as AdaGN(h, y) = ys * GroupNorm(h) + yb, with y derived from a projection of timestep and class embedding. However, the nature of this projection is underspecified. Specifically: are the timestep and class embedding concatenated and then projected? Is the timestep encoded (e.g., as in positional encoding) before concatenation? This is a standard design choice in diffusion models (e.g., using sinusoidal embeddings), but omitting it forces the reader to guess. While papers like Ho et al. [31] establish this convention, and the authors cite them, the omission in a methods-focused section reduces reproducibility. Moreover, the ablation shows a 2.02 FID drop when removing AdaGN—making it a key component—so precise specification is warranted. This is especially important because AdaGN differs from AdaGN in StyleGAN or FiLM in how embeddings are processed. While experts might infer the correct setup, the description falls short of full methodological transparency.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Algorithm 2's Intermediate Variable Notation
comments.explanation: The notation in Algorithm 2 is heavily corrupted by OCR and informal shorthand, making it difficult to parse key expressions. For example, 'ˆ ¯ _✏' appears to represent a modified noise estimate, likely $ \hat{\bar{\epsilon}} = \epsilon_\theta(x_t) - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \cdot s \cdot \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t) $, where guidance gradients are used to adjust the denoised prediction. The expression for $ \bar{x}_{t-1} $ seems to be a typo or corruption — possibly meant to be $ \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \left( \frac{x_t - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \hat{\bar{\epsilon}}}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}} \right) $, but the current form '¯ _xt−[p]_ 1 _−↵_ ¯ _t✏_ ˆ' is malformed. The final line 'ˆ _xt−_ 1 _[p] ↵t−_ 1 ⇣ ~~_p_~~ _↵_ ¯ _t_ ⌘ + _[p]_ 1 _− ↵t−_ 1 _✏_' likely intends: $ x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}} \left( \frac{x_t - \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \hat{\epsilon}}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}} \hat{\epsilon} $, a standard DDIM update. However, the use of '~~_p_~~' likely stems from OCR failure of $ \sqrt{\cdot} $ symbols, and '[p]' may be OCR for '\sqrt'. While the paper's context (DDIM, classifier guidance) suggests the intended derivation, the expression is so corrupted that a reader cannot reconstruct the update rule without external knowledge. This is critical because Algorithm 2 is central to the method (guided DDIM sampling), and reproduction depends on correctly parsing this update.

comments.title: Missing Square Root in Guided DDIM Gradient Term
comments.explanation: The update for $ \hat{\bar{\epsilon}} $ appears to be $ \epsilon_\theta(x_t) - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \cdot s \cdot \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t) $. However, the OCR output '−[p] 1 − ↵t' suggests a missing square root: it should be $ \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} $, not $ 1 - \bar{\alpha}_t $. This is a known factor in DDIM-style updates, where the gradient guidance is scaled by the standard deviation of the noise schedule at each step. The absence of the square root would significantly distort the gradient's magnitude. Given that the authors reference Song et al. [67] and Appendix D.3 for the score-based derivation, it's likely that the intended term includes $ \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} $, but the OCR failure omits the square root symbol (possibly 'p' is OCR for '\sqrt'). Still, since this affects the scaling of guidance and thus the algorithm's behavior, and no clarification is provided in the text, this constitutes a real error in the presented formula.

comments.title: Unclear Index in DDIM Update: $ \alpha_{t-1} $ vs $ \bar{\alpha}_{t-1} $
comments.explanation: The final update uses $ \alpha_{t-1} $, but in DDIM notation, $ \bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^t \alpha_s $ represents the cumulative product. The correct DDIM update should use $ \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} $ and $ \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} $. The current notation '↵t−1' suggests $ \alpha_{t-1} $, the single-step coefficient, not the cumulative one. This would be incorrect and lead to sampling errors. However, given the context and standard DDIM equations, it's highly likely that '↵t−1' is meant to denote $ \bar{\alpha}_{t-1} $, especially since earlier in the paper the cumulative $ \bar{\alpha}_t $ is referenced. The ambiguity arises because the bar notation may have been lost in OCR. Nonetheless, without explicit clarification, a reader might misinterpret the index level, leading to implementation errors.

comments.title: Missing Parentheses and Misplaced Terms in Algorithm 2
comments.explanation: The expression '⇣ ~~_p_~~ _↵_ ¯ _t_ ⌘' appears to isolate $ \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} $ in parentheses, which suggests that $ \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \cdot \left( \frac{x_t}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}} \right) $ is intended, but the full numerator $ x_t - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \hat{\bar{\epsilon}} $ is missing. The current form implies $ x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \cdot \left( \frac{x_t}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \hat{\bar{\epsilon}} $, which is incorrect — the $ \hat{\bar{\epsilon}} $ term must also be subtracted inside the first term. This is a significant error: omitting the noise correction in the normalized direction distorts the denoising path. The intended DDIM update is $ x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \left( \frac{x_t - \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \hat{\bar{\epsilon}}}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}} \right) + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \hat{\bar{\epsilon}} $. The OCR corruption completely breaks the structure of this expression, making recovery nearly impossible without consulting external sources. This level of corruption undermines the algorithm’s reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of $ \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t, t) $ vs $ \nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y|x_t) $
comments.quote: _pφ_ ( _y|xt, t_ ) = _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ) and _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) = _✏✓_ ( _xt_ ) for brevity, noting that they refer to separate functions for each timestep _t_ and at training time the models must be conditioned on the input _t_.
comments.explanation: The authors define the classifier as $ p_\phi(y|x_t, t) $, explicitly depending on timestep $ t $, and correctly state that in practice they condition on $ t $. However, they then write $ p_\phi(y|x_t, t) = p_\phi(y|x_t) $ for brevity, which is misleading — it suggests the classifier is time-independent, which contradicts the earlier assumption. A more precise statement would be: 'we omit the explicit $ t $-dependence in notation for brevity, but the classifier is trained and evaluated with $ t $ as input'. As written, a reader might assume the classifier is shared across timesteps, which would be unusual and potentially harmful (since noise levels vary drastically). However, the clarification that 'they refer to separate functions for each timestep $ t $' resolves this issue. So while the equality is notationally inaccurate, the prose explanation prevents misunderstanding. Still, overloading notation this way risks confusion.

comments.title: Unclear Justification for Gradient Scaling > 1
comments.quote: In initial experiments with unconditional ImageNet models, we found it necessary to scale the classifier gradients by a constant factor larger than 1. ... Scaling up the classifier gradients remedied this problem, and the class probabilities from the classifier increased to nearly 100%.
comments.explanation: The justification for using gradient scales $ s > 1 $ rests on two observations: (1) samples become more class-consistent visually, and (2) the classifier assigns higher confidence post-hoc. The theoretical argument — that scaling gradients corresponds to exponentiating the classifier distribution — is sound (proportional to $ p(y|x)^s $). However, the issue is that classifier confidence on generated samples is not a ground truth measure of correctness. The paper lacks a control experiment showing that increased confidence correlates with actual class accuracy (e.g. via human evaluation or a held-out classifier). It's possible that scaling simply pushes samples into regions the classifier is overconfident about, without true semantic alignment. While this is a common practice in classifier guidance (e.g., in CLIP-guided generation), the paper presents it as definitive evidence. This is a minor overclaim: the method works empirically (as shown in Figure 2 and Table 3), but the justification based solely on classifier confidence is circular.

comments.title: Missing Specification of Gradient Scaling Schedule
comments.quote: scaling the classifier gradients by a constant factor larger than 1
comments.explanation: The paper states that gradients are scaled by a constant $ s $, but does not specify whether this is applied uniformly across all timesteps or varies with $ t $. In later works (e.g., ADM, DALL-E), timestep-dependent guidance scales (e.g., higher weights in mid-noise regimes) are used. The omission here makes reproduction ambiguous. While constant scaling is the simplest approach and plausible, the absence of this detail — especially in Algorithm 2, where $ s $ appears globally — leaves the method underspecified. Given that guidance effectiveness can depend on noise level, this is a meaningful omission. However, since the paper refers to 'gradient scale $ s $' as a scalar input to the algorithm, constant scaling is implied, mitigating the issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of classifier-guided DDIM sampling in Algorithm 2
comments.quote:
**Algorithm 2** Classifier guided DDIM sampling, given a diffusion model _✏✓_ ( _xt_ ), classifier _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ), and gradient scale _s_ .

Input: class label _y_ , gradient scale _s xT_ sample from _N_ (0 _,_ **I** ) **for all** _t_ from _T_ to 1 **do** ˆ ¯ _✏ ✏✓_ ( _xt_ ) _−[p]_ 1 _− ↵t rxt_ log _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ) ¯ _xt−[p]_ 1 _−↵_ ¯ _t✏_ ˆ ¯ ˆ _xt−_ 1 _[p] ↵t−_ 1 ⇣ ~~_p_~~ _↵_ ¯ _t_ ⌘ + _[p]_ 1 _− ↵t−_ 1 _✏_

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'ADM (dropout)' notation across tables
comments.explanation: The notation 'ADM (dropout)' appears in multiple rows of Table 4 (e.g., LSUN Bedrooms, LSUN Cats, ImageNet 64×64), but is not clearly defined in the text. In the paper summary and earlier sections, 'ADM' is defined as the ablated diffusion model without classifier guidance. However, 'ADM (dropout)' is never explicitly defined. While the name suggests that dropout was used, no mention is made of this architectural choice in Section 3, where ablations are discussed. Table 1 mentions residual rescaling, attention, and AdaGN, but not dropout. This raises confusion: is 'ADM (dropout)' a variant with dropout added, or is it a typo/mislabeling? Given that 'ADM' and 'ADM (dropout)' appear as separate entries (e.g., LSUN Horses: ADM vs ADM (dropout)), they must represent different models. However, without explanation, a reader cannot determine whether dropout improves performance or if 'ADM (dropout)' is actually the best configuration. The fact that 'ADM (dropout)' outperforms 'ADM' in some LSUN tasks but underperforms in ImageNet 256×256 (FID 10.94 vs 10.94) adds to the confusion. Context does not resolve this—dropout is not mentioned in the methodology. This underspecification makes reproduction difficult.

comments.title: Inconsistent FID values between Table 4 and abstract
comments.quote: We achieve an FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128, 4.59 on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256, and 7.72 on ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512
comments.explanation: The abstract claims an FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 128×128, but in Table 4, the entry for 'ADM-G' on ImageNet 128×128 reports FID = 2.97, which matches. For 256×256, the abstract again reports 4.59, and Table 4 confirms this for 'ADM-G'. For 512×512, the abstract says 7.72, and Table 4 shows 'ADM-G' with FID = 7.72. So at first glance, consistency appears. However, later the abstract states: 'improving FID to 3.94 on ImageNet 256×256 and 3.85 on ImageNet 512×512'—which corresponds to the 'ADM-G, ADM-U' rows in Table 5, i.e., guided plus upsampling. But Table 4 only lists 'ADM-G', not the upsampling variant. The issue is not numerical inconsistency, but presentation: Table 4 is titled 'Sample quality comparison with state-of-the-art generative models' and includes only base and guided models, yet the abstract's final numbers refer to a *further improved* model not shown in Table 4. While the upsampling results are shown in Table 5, the absence of a unified comparison table that includes upsampling variants is misleading. A reader might assume Table 4 contains the best results, but the best FIDs (3.94, 3.85) are only in Table 5. This could lead to underestimation of the method's performance when relying solely on Table 4.

comments.title: Missing justification for gradient scaling equivalence to renormalized distribution
comments.quote: note that _s · rx_ log _p_ ( _y|x_ ) = _rx_ log _Z_[1] _[p]_[(] _[y][|][x]_[)] _[s]_[,][where] _[Z]_[is][an][arbitrary][constant.][As][a] proportional to _p_ ( _y|x_ ) _[s]_ .
comments.explanation: The text asserts that scaling the gradient $s \nabla_{x} \log p(y|x)$ is equivalent to taking the gradient of $\log p(y|x)^s$, which is correct. It then concludes that this corresponds to sampling from a distribution proportional to $p(y|x)^s$. While mathematically sound in the limit of small steps (as in Langevin dynamics), this connection is not trivial and requires justification beyond the gradient identity. The derivation assumes that the guidance process implicitly defines a modified score function that corresponds to a new conditional distribution $p'(y|x) \propto p(y|x)^s$, but this holds only under specific conditions (e.g., when the guidance is applied consistently across all timesteps and the classifier is well-calibrated). The paper does not discuss potential calibration issues or the effect of $s$ on classifier overconfidence. Moreover, the claim that this 'sharpens' the effective distribution assumes independence between $p_\phi(y|x_t)$ and the diffusion model, which may not hold under noise. While the intuition is correct and widely accepted (e.g., in energy-based models), the lack of discussion around assumptions or limitations (e.g., classifier quality, noise-level dependence) makes this a hand-waved justification. The core claim is valid, but the reasoning is underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguity in sampling steps for 'ADM-G (25 steps)' entries
comments.quote: ImageNet diffusion models are sampled using 250 steps, except when we use the DDIM sampler with 25 steps.
comments.explanation: The text states that 25 steps are used when 'we use the DDIM sampler'. However, DDIM is not exclusive to 25 steps—it's a sampler that can be run with any number of steps. The phrasing suggests that DDIM is only used for 25 steps, which is misleading. In reality, DDIM can be and often is used with other step counts. The intended meaning is likely that 'when using 25 steps, we employ the DDIM sampler', which is reasonable. But as written, it implies a one-to-one correspondence between DDIM and 25 steps, which is incorrect. This could mislead a reader into thinking the 25-step models are the only ones using DDIM, when in fact it may be used throughout. Fortunately, context from Section 2 clarifies that DDIM is used 'when using fewer than 50 sampling steps', so the issue is resolved upon deeper reading. Still, the phrasing in the caption is imprecise and could cause momentary confusion.

comments.title: Classifier guidance application in Table 3 unclear for model types
comments.quote: Table 3: Effect of classifier guidance on sample quality. Both conditional and unconditional models were trained for 2M iterations on ImageNet 256 ⇥ 256 with batch size 256.
comments.explanation: At first glance, Table 3 presents results for both 'conditional' and 'unconditional' models with varying guidance scales, but the labeling of rows (e.g., '7 7', '7 3', etc.) is unclear without reference to the full table context. In particular, the row labels (like '7 7') appear to refer to architectural configurations or hyperparameters not defined in the provided passage. While one might expect these to be combinations of model width and depth based on Table 1, the absence of clear mapping raises concerns about reproducibility. However, given that Tables 1 and 2 do define architectural choices, and the paper likely defines these labelings elsewhere (Appendix A.1), this is a forward reference issue. The actual concern lies in the ambiguity of how exactly classifier guidance is applied to the 'unconditional' model: guidance typically requires a classifier trained on noisy images, which is mentioned, but the table implies guidance is being applied post-training, which is correct and consistent with the method description. Still, the phrasing 'both conditional and unconditional models' might mislead a reader into thinking that guidance is trained into the model rather than applied at sampling time. The method is technically sound—as described in Algorithm 1 and 2—but the tabulation risks implying a training-time modification. This is a mild framing ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistent mention of 'ADM-G' in final claim vs prior capability
comments.quote: Finally, by combining guidance with upsampling, we can further improve sample quality on high-resolution conditional image synthesis.
comments.explanation: In the Conclusion section, the authors state that combining guidance with upsampling improves sample quality. This aligns with the earlier claim in the PAPER SUMMARY (and abstract) that ADM-G combined with ADM-U achieves FID 3.85. However, in the Conclusion, the model is not explicitly named (e.g., as 'ADM-G-U' or similar), and 'ADM-G' is used throughout the body as the guided version of the ablated diffusion model. The key issue is that prior text claims 'combining both (ADM-G, ADM-U) achieves the best FID', but this combination is never formally defined or named in the provided sections. While it is implied that ADM-G refers only to classifier guidance and ADM-U to upsampling, and their combination is additive, the lack of a unified designation or architectural description for how the upsampling stage conditions on guided low-res outputs could lead to ambiguity. However, since the conditioning method (channel-wise concatenation after interpolation) is defined, and guidance is applied only to the lower-resolution model per assumptions, the method is sufficiently specified. This is a minor underspecification, resolvable from context, but a careful reader might momentarily question whether the upsampling model itself uses guidance too, which the assumptions rule out.

comments.title: Misleading implication about classifier guidance scaling in FID trade-off
comments.quote: We see that scaling the gradients beyond 1.0 smoothly trades off recall (a measure of diversity) for higher precision and IS (measures of fidelity).
comments.explanation: The authors claim that gradient scaling smoothly trades off recall for precision and IS, which is supported by Figure 3 (not shown here but referenced). However, in Table 3, increasing guidance scale from 1.0 to 10.0 in conditional models leads to a drop in recall from 0.63 to 0.32 and a rise in precision from 0.69 to 0.88. While this trend supports the trade-off claim, the phrasing 'smoothly trades off' might imply a monotonic and controllable balance, whereas in practice, very high scales may oversharpen the classifier posterior, leading to mode collapse or implausible samples not reflected in standard metrics. The paper acknowledges saturation in precision-recall trade-off (PAPER SUMMARY), so the claim is not overreaching. The behavior is well-documented in classifier guidance literature (e.g., DDIM, Song et al.), and the term 'smoothly' is likely used informally. Thus, while a careful reader might initially interpret this as implying infinite scalability of quality with scale, the results in Table 3 and the discussion in Section 4 resolve that concern by showing diminishing gains and recall drop. The statement remains slightly imprecise but not misleading in context.

comments.title: Ambiguity in classifier normalization layer description
comments.quote: Our classifier architecture is simply the downsampling trunk of the UNet model with an attention pool [55] at the 8x8 layer to produce the final output.
comments.explanation: The passage states that the classifier uses the 'downsampling trunk' of the UNet with an 'attention pool' at 8x8. However, 'attention pool' is not standard terminology—it likely refers to an attention-based global pooling operation, such as that used in Vision Transformers or Zhang et al. [55] (CLIP), where attention aggregates features. While CLIP does use attention pooling, and [55] is cited, it's unclear whether the operation is average pooling with attention weights, a learned pooling head, or something else. This could affect reproducibility: a reader might not know whether to use standard global average pooling or a more complex module. That said, since the reference is to the CLIP architecture, which uses attention pooling as defined in Radford et al., and the authors say 'similar to recent methods', the method is indirectly specified. Still, for a standalone diffusion paper, 'attention pool' is underspecified. A more precise term like 'attention-based global pooling' or a brief equation would help. This is particularly important because classifier quality directly affects guidance performance.

comments.title: Temporal inconsistency in claimed FID values
comments.quote: We achieve an FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 128 ⇥ 128, 4.59 on ImageNet 256 ⇥ 256, and 7.72 on ImageNet 512 ⇥ 512
comments.explanation: This statement appears in the Abstract and is repeated in Section 5. However, the PAPER SUMMARY (which likely reflects the final results) states that combining ADM-G and ADM-U achieves even better FIDs: 3.94 and 3.85, respectively. This creates a contradiction: is the 4.59 FID the standalone ADM-G result, or is it superseded by the combined model? Checking context resolves this—ADM-G refers to the base guided model, while the 3.94 and 3.85 are for the combined guidance + upsampling setup. The Abstract claims state-of-the-art results but leads with the standalone model numbers. However, this risks a reader believing that 4.59 is the best result on 256x256, when actually 3.94 is. This is not a mathematical error but a presentational one: the best result should be highlighted. However, the Abstract does mention that combining guidance with upsampling further improves FID to 3.85, so the information is present. The phrasing is therefore slightly ambiguous but not incorrect. A major work like this justifies prioritizing clarity in ranking claims, so this remains a minor issue.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper makes a highly significant contribution to generative modeling by rigorously demonstrating that diffusion models can outperform GANs in sample quality while retaining superior distribution coverage and training stability. The writing is exceptionally clear and well-motivated, effectively articulating the limitations of existing likelihood-based models and GANs to position the authors' two core contributions—architectural refinement via ablation and the introduction of classifier guidance—as natural and impactful solutions. The reported state-of-the-art results on ImageNet are impressive and strongly support the abstract's claims of beating GANs. However, the primary concerns for the full review center on the computational efficiency and practical deployability of the proposed methods; while the abstract highlights efficiency relative to prior diffusion work, the iterative sampling nature of these models and the additional gradient computations required by classifier guidance pose a substantial overhead compared to the single-pass inference of GANs that must be quantified rigorously. Furthermore, the reliance on classifier guidance introduces a dependency on an external classifier, raising questions regarding training stability, potential bias amplification, and the scaling of guidance strength, all of which warrant detailed analysis. Finally, while FID scores are compelling, the authors should ensure comprehensive evaluation with diversity-focused metrics to fully validate the claim of matching BigGAN-deep's fidelity without sacrificing coverage, given the known deficiencies of FID in capturing distributional nuances.

comments.title: Reference [2] mismatch / unresolved draft placeholder
comments.quote:
body of work [23, 53, 2] aims to optimize GAN latent spaces for text prompts using pre-trained CLIP [55] models.

[2] Adverb. The big sleep. `https://twitter.com/advadnoun/status/ 1351038053033406468` , 2021.
comments.explanation: The text cites [2] as part of a "body of work" exploring GAN latent space optimization with CLIP. However, the reference list identifies [2] as a personal tweet by @advadnoun rather than an academic publication. In the original NeurIPS 2021 submission, [2] corresponds to "Improved diffusion models" (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). This appears to be an OCR extraction artifact or an unresolved author placeholder from an earlier draft. While it does not undermine the paper's mathematical or methodological claims, it creates a citation inconsistency that would mislead a reader attempting to locate the intended source. Correcting the reference entry to match the cited work would resolve this.

comments.title: Misaligned citations for generative-model-specific fake news detection
comments.quote: detecting and mitigating propaganda and fake news based on generative models is an ongoing area of research [4, 3, 5].
comments.explanation: The authors claim that detecting fake news "based on generative models" is an active research area and support it with references [4], [3], and [5]. Upon checking the reference list, [4] and [5] describe traditional text-classification pipelines using N-grams and shallow ML for fake news detection, which predate and are distinct from challenges posed by generative AI. Reference [3] addresses deepfakes (visual media) but focuses on protecting specific individuals rather than methodological advances in generative-model detection. The citation cluster does not directly support the claim about generative-model-specific detection. A few citations targeting watermarking, adversarial detectors for diffusion/GAN outputs, or LLM-generated text detection would better align with the stated claim.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is an exemplary piece of technical academic writing, characterized by a clear, logical progression that effectively bridges the gap between foundational theory and empirical innovation. The authors exhibit high scholarly quality by explicitly identifying the limitations of the current state-of-the-art—namely, the instability and limited diversity of GANs—and articulating a rigorous hypothesis for why diffusion models have historically lagged behind in fidelity. The clarity of the exposition is particularly notable; the transition from theoretical background to the "classifier guidance" methodology provides a compelling narrative that motivates their architectural improvements. The most significant strength of the work is this dual-pronged approach, which addresses both the structural capacity of the model and the trade-off between distribution coverage and sample fidelity. Given the provided excerpt, the paper succeeds in establishing a strong, verifiable baseline and a clear research trajectory, suggesting an impactful contribution to the generative modeling landscape.

comments.title: OCR-induced notation error in Algorithm 2
comments.explanation: The text in Algorithm 2 contains significant corruption from the OCR process. While the context of DDIM (from Song et al. [64]) makes it clear that the authors intend to describe the standard sampling update step, the expression as written is mathematically incoherent due to jumbled labels and broken delimiters (e.g., '[p]'). A reader attempting to implement this based strictly on the provided text would be unable to do so.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for variance interpolation
comments.quote: ⌃ ✓ ( xt, t ) = exp( v log βt + (1 − v ) log β[˜] t )
comments.explanation: The OCR has rendered the variance notation as '⌃'. In standard diffusion model literature, this symbol is reserved for the covariance matrix Σ. Given the context of scalar interpolation between β_t and β_tilde_t, the symbol '⌃' is clearly a misread of 'Σ'. While this is an OCR error, the misuse of this symbol across the paper creates potential confusion for readers attempting to verify the math against the cited Equation 5.

comments.title: Incomplete definition of AdaGN
comments.quote: AdaGN( h, y ) = ys GroupNorm( h )+ yb
comments.explanation: The definition of AdaGN is underspecified regarding the dimensions and broadcast behavior. The variables 'ys' and 'yb' are described as coming from a linear projection, but the definition does not specify the spatial vs. channel-wise application. While this is common in deep learning papers, for a core architectural innovation claiming to provide a 'substantial boost', the lack of explicit dimension handling (whether these are scalar shifts/scales per channel or spatial tensors) makes the reproduction of the exact operation ambiguous.
